question: [File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setRoleSetter] Can an unprivileged borrower call setRoleSetter(address(0)) when the current roleSetter has already been set to address(0) via a prior legitimate call, under the precondition that roleSetter == address(0) and msg.sender == address(0) is unreachable, triggering a permanent lock of all role-management functions (setFeeSetter, setFeeClaimer, setTickSpacingSetter), violating the invariant that protocol roles must always be reassignable by a live address, causing scoped impact: permanent fund freeze of all unclaimed fees and inability to update fee/tick parameters? Proof idea: unit test setRoleSetter(address(0)) then assert every subsequent setFeeSetter/setFeeClaimer/setTickSpacingSetter call reverts with OnlyRoleSetter().

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setFeeClaimer / claimSettlementFee] Can an unprivileged attacker front-run a pending setFeeClaimer(newClaimer) transaction by observing the mempool, then as the old feeClaimer immediately call claimSettlementFee(token, claimableSettlementFee[token], attacker) to drain all accrued settlement fees before the role transfer completes, under the precondition that claimableSettlementFee[token] > 0 and the old feeClaimer is attacker-controlled, violating the invariant that fee assets must only flow to the intended protocol recipient, causing scoped impact: direct loss of all accrued settlement fees? Proof idea: fork test with mempool ordering; assert claimableSettlementFee[token] == 0 after front-run and new claimer receives nothing.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / setRoleSetter] Can an authorized operator (isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true) call setIsAuthorized(attacker2, true, victim) to transitively grant a third address full control over victim's Midnight state, then have attacker2 call setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) again after victim revokes attacker, under the precondition that victim authorized attacker for a limited purpose, violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure of victim's collateral and credit via withdrawCollateral/withdraw? Proof idea: state-transition test; authorize attacker, attacker grants attacker2, victim revokes attacker, assert attacker2 still has access and can drain position.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: multicall / setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use multicall to atomically call setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, attacker) (self-authorization no-op) followed by a second call that exploits the isAuthorized mapping being set in the same transaction context, under the precondition that multicall uses delegatecall and msg.sender is preserved across all calls, violating the invariant that multicall must not bypass per-action invariants, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption if any function incorrectly reads stale authorization state mid-multicall? Proof idea: fuzz multicall call ordering with setIsAuthorized as first element; assert no position state changes for unauthorized users.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: claimContinuousFee] Can the feeClaimer call claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) with amount > marketState[id].continuousFeeCredit, under the precondition that continuousFeeCredit < withdrawable (lender funds are present), causing the subtraction continuousFeeCredit -= amount to underflow (Solidity 0.8 reverts) or, if the cast via UtilsLib.toUint128 truncates silently, to corrupt the continuousFeeCredit field, violating the invariant that total claimable credit must not exceed repaid loan assets, causing scoped impact: protocol insolvency where lenders cannot withdraw their share? Proof idea: unit test claimContinuousFee with amount == continuousFeeCredit + 1; assert revert or assert withdrawable and totalUnits remain consistent.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketContinuousFee / take] Can the feeSetter call setMarketContinuousFee(id, MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE) immediately before a large lender's take, under the precondition that the lender's credit is about to increase significantly, causing the new lender's pendingFee to be computed at the maximum rate while existing lenders retain their lower crystallized rate, violating the invariant that continuous fee must be fixed for a lender at position entry, causing scoped impact: disproportionate fee extraction from new lenders compared to existing ones, effectively a targeted fee attack? Proof idea: differential test; compare pendingFee accrual for lender entering before vs after setMarketContinuousFee; assert existing lender's pendingFee is unchanged.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setDefaultSettlementFee / touchMarket] Can the feeSetter call setDefaultSettlementFee(loanToken, index, maxSettlementFee(index)) just before a market creator calls touchMarket(market) for a new market with that loanToken, under the precondition that the market creator expects zero fees (defaultSettlementFeeCbp was 0), causing the new market to inherit maximum settlement fees at creation, violating the invariant that market config is immutable after creation but the fee was set before creation, causing scoped impact: takers in the new market pay unexpectedly high fees, constituting a market parameter setter access-control failure? Proof idea: state-transition test; set default fee, create market, assert settlementFeeCbp values match the manipulated defaults.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: claimSettlementFee] Can the feeClaimer call claimSettlementFee(token, amount, receiver) where receiver is a malicious ERC20-callback contract (ERC777 hook or similar), under the precondition that the loan token implements a tokensReceived hook that re-enters claimSettlementFee before claimableSettlementFee[token] is decremented in a second call, violating the invariant that ERC20 transfer deltas must match accounting deltas, causing scoped impact: double-claim of settlement fees draining protocol token balance? Proof idea: reentrancy PoC where receiver.tokensReceived calls claimSettlementFee again; assert claimableSettlementFee[token] is not double-decremented and token balance is consistent.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketTickSpacing] Can the tickSpacingSetter call setMarketTickSpacing(id, 1) to reduce tick spacing to 1 (the minimum divisor of any DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING), under the precondition that DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING is a composite number, causing all integer ticks to become accessible and enabling offers at ticks that were previously invalid, violating the invariant that finer ticks keep previous ticks valid but should not open ticks that break price quantization assumptions, causing scoped impact: offer manipulation at newly accessible ticks that produce rounding artifacts in settlement fee or asset calculations? Proof idea: fuzz tick values between old and new spacing; assert settlementFee and asset computations remain within expected bounds after spacing reduction.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / repay] Can an authorized operator call repay(market, units, victim, callback, data) on behalf of victim with a malicious callback address, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and the callback is attacker-controlled, causing the callback to observe victim's reduced debt state before the token transfer and re-enter setIsAuthorized to revoke victim's other operators, violating the invariant that callbacks must not corrupt partial state through reentrancy, causing scoped impact: authorization corruption leaving victim unable to manage their own position? Proof idea: reentrancy test in onRepay callback calling setIsAuthorized(legitimateOperator, false, victim); assert authorization state is consistent post-repay.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setFeeSetter / setMarketSettlementFee] Can the roleSetter set feeSetter to a contract that atomically calls setMarketSettlementFee for all 7 breakpoint indices in a single transaction, under the precondition that the new feeSetter is a smart contract with no access controls, allowing any caller to trigger fee changes via the feeSetter contract's public function, violating the invariant that only the feeSetter address should be able to modify fees, causing scoped impact: unauthorized fee manipulation by any unprivileged user through the feeSetter proxy contract? Proof idea: deploy feeSetter contract with public setFee function; assert unprivileged user can call it and change market fees.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / setConsumed] Can an authorized operator call setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel all of victim's offers in a group, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and victim has active maker offers in that group, violating the invariant that offer cancellation must only be performed by the maker or their intended delegate, causing scoped impact: offer DoS where victim's liquidity is permanently cancelled without consent? Proof idea: unit test; authorize attacker, attacker calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim), assert victim's offers can no longer be filled.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / withdrawCollateral] Can an authorized operator call withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, victim, attacker) to redirect victim's collateral to the attacker's address, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and victim has collateral deposited, violating the invariant that user funds must not be stolen outside valid protocol rules, causing scoped impact: direct loss of victim's collateral assets to attacker? Proof idea: unit test; authorize attacker, call withdrawCollateral with receiver=attacker, assert attacker receives collateral tokens.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: claimContinuousFee / withdraw] Can the feeClaimer call claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) where amount equals the entire withdrawable balance, under the precondition that lenders have pending withdrawals and continuousFeeCredit == withdrawable, causing lenders' subsequent withdraw calls to revert due to insufficient withdrawable balance, violating the invariant that exit paths must remain available even when enter gate reverts, causing scoped impact: permanent fund freeze for lenders who cannot withdraw their credit? Proof idea: invariant test; after claimContinuousFee drains withdrawable, assert sum of all lender credit <= withdrawable.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketSettlementFee / take] Can the feeSetter call setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, 0) to zero out the 0d breakpoint fee, then setMarketSettlementFee(id, 6, maxSettlementFee(6)) to maximize the 360d breakpoint, under the precondition that a taker is about to execute a take near maturity boundary (TTM near 0), causing the settlement fee computation to interpolate between 0 and max in a way that produces unexpected fee amounts due to piecewise linear edge cases, violating the invariant that settlement fee must be bounded by the annualized cap, causing scoped impact: fee overclaim or underclaim at TTM boundary? Proof idea: fuzz TTM values near 0, 1d, and 7d breakpoints with manipulated fee arrays; assert settlementFee(id, ttm) stays within documented caps.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / take] Can an authorized operator call take(offer, ratifierData, units, victim, receiver, takerCallback, takerCallbackData) on behalf of victim as taker, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and the offer is a sell offer that increases victim's debt, causing victim to accumulate debt without consent while attacker receives the loan tokens via receiver parameter, violating the invariant that every borrow/take must create correct debt and collateral obligation with user consent, causing scoped impact: unauthorized debt creation and direct loss of collateral? Proof idea: unit test; authorize attacker, attacker calls take with victim as taker and attacker as receiver; assert victim's debt increased and attacker received loan tokens.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setRoleSetter] Can the current roleSetter call setRoleSetter(roleSetter) (self-assignment) as a no-op, then in the same multicall batch call setFeeSetter(attacker), under the precondition that multicall preserves msg.sender as roleSetter across all delegatecall frames, violating the invariant that only the roleSetter should be able to modify roles, causing scoped impact: if multicall somehow changes msg.sender context, an unprivileged user could set feeSetter? Proof idea: unit test multicall([setRoleSetter(self), setFeeSetter(attacker)]); assert only roleSetter can execute this sequence and msg.sender is correctly preserved.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setDefaultContinuousFee / touchMarket] Can the feeSetter call setDefaultContinuousFee(loanToken, MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE) between two market creations for the same loanToken, under the precondition that a second market with the same loanToken but different collateral params is about to be created, causing the second market to inherit MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE while the first market retains its original lower fee, violating the invariant that market config is immutable after creation but default fees affect new markets, causing scoped impact: new lenders in the second market pay maximum continuous fees while lenders in the first market do not? Proof idea: create market1 with fee=0, set default to MAX, create market2, assert market2.continuousFee == MAX and market1.continuousFee == 0.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: claimSettlementFee] Can the feeClaimer call claimSettlementFee(token, 0, receiver) as a no-op that still emits a ClaimSettlementFee event with amount=0, under the precondition that off-chain monitoring systems use event logs to track fee claims, causing accounting desync between on-chain state and off-chain fee tracking systems, violating the invariant that ERC20 transfer deltas must match accounting deltas (0 transfer with 0 accounting change is consistent but misleading), causing scoped impact: accounting corruption in off-chain systems that rely on event-based fee tracking? Proof idea: unit test claimSettlementFee(token, 0, receiver); assert claimableSettlementFee[token] unchanged and no token transfer occurred.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / liquidate] Can an authorized operator call liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, 0, victim, false, attacker, address(0), '') on behalf of no one (liquidate is permissionless), under the precondition that victim is unhealthy and isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true is irrelevant since liquidate does not check authorization, causing attacker to liquidate victim and receive collateral via receiver=attacker even though victim's authorized operator could have repaid the debt, violating the invariant that only unhealthy accounts should be liquidatable, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure of collateral from a position that an authorized operator was about to make healthy? Proof idea: race condition test; assert liquidate succeeds before authorized repay in same block.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketTickSpacing / take] Can the tickSpacingSetter call setMarketTickSpacing(id, newSpacing) where newSpacing divides the current spacing but does not divide the tick of an existing resting offer, under the precondition that a maker has a signed offer at tick T where T % newSpacing != 0, causing the offer to become inaccessible (TickNotAccessible revert) even though it was valid at creation, violating the invariant that finer ticks keep previous ticks valid, causing scoped impact: offer DoS where maker's liquidity is permanently frozen? Proof idea: create offer at tick=2, reduce spacing to 3 (if DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=6), assert take reverts with TickNotAccessible.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / withdraw] Can an authorized operator call withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker) to redirect victim's credit redemption to attacker's address, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and victim has withdrawable credit, violating the invariant that user funds must not be stolen outside valid protocol rules, causing scoped impact: direct loss of victim's loan token assets to attacker? Proof idea: unit test; authorize attacker, call withdraw with onBehalf=victim and receiver=attacker; assert attacker receives loan tokens.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setFeeClaimer / claimContinuousFee] Can the roleSetter set feeClaimer to address(0), under the precondition that there are accrued continuous fees in continuousFeeCredit, causing all future claimContinuousFee calls to revert with OnlyFeeClaimer() since msg.sender can never equal address(0), violating the invariant that protocol fees must be claimable, causing scoped impact: permanent fund freeze of all accrued continuous fees locked in the contract? Proof idea: unit test setFeeClaimer(address(0)); assert claimContinuousFee reverts and continuousFeeCredit is permanently locked.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setMarketSettlementFee / claimSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter call setMarketSettlementFee(id, index, 0) for all 7 indices to zero out all settlement fees for a market, under the precondition that claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] > 0 from prior takes, causing future takes in that market to contribute 0 to claimableSettlementFee while the feeClaimer can still claim the previously accrued amount, violating the invariant that fees must be correctly attributed to the right market and period, causing scoped impact: fee underclaim for future takers and potential accounting desync? Proof idea: take with nonzero fee, zero all fees, take again, assert second take contributes 0 to claimableSettlementFee.

[File: 'src/interfaces/IERC20.sol -> Scope: High Access-control failure in fee setter, role setter, tick-spacing setter, fee claimer, market parameter setter, or authorization setter'] [Function: setIsAuthorized / supplyCollateral] Can an authorized operator call supplyCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, victim) where the collateral token is a fee-on-transfer token, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true and the collateral token charges a fee, causing victim's collateral[collateralIndex] to be credited with assets but only assets*(1-fee) tokens actually transferred, violating the invariant that ERC20 transfer deltas must match accounting deltas, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where victim's recorded collateral exceeds actual token balance? Proof idea: fuzz fee-on-transfer collateral token; assert position.collateral[i] == actual tokens received by contract.
